Facts.text: Alfonso VI of León and Castile captures the Muslim taifa of Toledo
First attack on the Jewish community of Frankfurt am Main, Germany
Henry the Navigator is appointed governor of the Order of Christ
Holy Roman EmperorCharles Vreturns to Spain
English merchant ship Tryall wrecks on the Montebello Islands, north western Australia - first English to land on Australian soil and the country's oldest European shipwreck
Great Britain and Holy Roman Empire sign the Treaty of Westminster, a defensive pact
The Ship "Le Grand St Antoine" reaches Marseille, bringing Europe's last major plague outbreak. Kills around 100,000.
John Copson becomes America's 1st insurance agent
A treaty between Pennsylvania and Maryland ends the Conojocular War with settlement of a boundary dispute and exchange of prisoners
Jews are expelled from Warsaw by Marshall Mniszek
Father Stephen Theodore Badin is 1st US Roman Catholic priest ordained
In the May Revolution, citizens of Buenos Aires expel the Spanish Viceroy Cisneros during Semana de Mayo
The Primera Junta (local government) is established in Argentina
American Unitarian Association founded
The Patriots of Lower Canada (Quebec) rebel against the British for freedom.
1st telegraphed news dispatch is published in Baltimore Patriot

John Merryman is arrested and imprisoned but not charged, during US Civil War suspension of writ of habeas corpus; case later sparks partial Supreme Court decision against suspension of writ on grounds it was illegally enacted, congress later enshrines the writ, making the decision moot
Australian Aboriginal Cricket tour of England begins v Surrey Gentlemen
Irish Fenians raid Eccles Hill, Quebec
1st tie in NL history (Athletics & Louisville, 2-2 in 14)
Gas lamp at Paris Opera catches fire; 200 die
The Republic of Formosa is formed, with Tang Ching-sung as the president
Retired London fire master Eyre Massey Shaw aged 70 reputedly becomes oldest gold medalist in Olympics for sailing (disputed)
After 20 straight wins, Boston Pilgrims lose to Chicago White Sox 3-0
British House of Commons passes the Irish Home Rule Bill
Chicago Whales' Claude Hendrix no-hits Pittsburgh Rebels (Federal League), 10-0
Second Battle of Ypres on the Western Front ends with 105,000 casualties
Britain recognizes Transjordan with Abdullah as its leader
Sholom Schwartzbard assassinates Symon Petliura, the head of the Paris-based government-in-exile of the Ukrainian People's Republic
Goofy, aka Dippy Dawg, 1st appears in 'Mickey's Revue' by Walt Disney
Spanish piano virtuoso José Iturbi makes his conducting debut, leading an orchestra at the Teatro Hidalgo in Mexico City, Mexico
Babe Ruthhits his last 3 home runs in Pittsburgh, Boston Braves still lose the game 11–7 to the Pirates
The Remington Rand strike, led by the American Federation of Labor, begins
1st airmail letter to circle globe returns to NY
Spanish Civil War: The bombing of Alicante takes place, with 313 deaths
Carl Storck becomes 2nd NFL president
German troops conquer Boulogne
Golden Gate International Exposition reopens
5,000 drown in a storm at Ganges Delta region in India
First commercial fluid catalytic cracking facility begins production at Exxon, developed by the "Four Horsemen” research team at Exxon. The process now produces half the world's gasoline.
Riot at Mobile, Alabama, shipyard over upgrading 12 black workers
Trident conference in Washington, D.C. (operation plan '43 against Japan)
Arthur C. Clark proposes relay satellites in geosynchronous orbit
Jordan gains independence from Britain (National Day); Abdullah ibn Hussein becomes King of Jordan
Coal dust explosion rocks Centralia Coal Co's Mine #5 killing 111 in Illinois
San Francisco receives its 1st telecast
Chinese Red army occupies Shanghai
Brooklyn Battery Tunnel opens in NYC
1st atomic cannon electronically fired at Frenchman Flat, Nevada
1st non-commercial educational television station - Houston, Texas
Braves Max Surkont strikes out record 8 Reds in a row
Series of 19 twisters destroy Udall, Kansas and most of Blackwell, Oklahoma
US Supreme Courtt rules Louisiana prohibiting black-white boxing unconstitutional
George Crowe's record 11th pinch-hit HR
US performs nuclear test at Christmas Island (atmospheric)
US unions AFL-CIO starts campaign for 35-hour work week
Wand Records releases The Isley Brothers' cover single "Twist & Shout"; it becomes their first top-20 hit, peaking at #17
Great Britain ends its amateur-professional classes in cricket
Organization for African Unity formed by Chad, Mauritania and Zambia
Ground is broken for new Busch Memorial Stadium in St Louis; opens in 1966, demolished in 2005
US Supreme Court rules closing schools to avoid desegregation is unconstitutional
Dave Davies of The Kinks knocked unconscious in an on stage scuffle with drummer Mick Avory at Cardiff’s Capital Theatre
Fighting along the Indian and Pakistan border
Roel van Duyn, Martijn Ananar & Rob Faado align in Provo
"Au Hasard Balthazar", French film directed by Robert Bresson, starring Anne Wiazemsky and François Lafarge, is released
Peru & Argentina soccer fans fight in Lima; 248 die
BPAA All-Star Bowling Tournament won by Jim Stefanich
BPAA US Women's Bowling Open won by Dotty Fothergill
Gateway Arch in St Louis dedicated
Rolling Stones release song "Jumping Jack Flash"
Unicorn by The Irish Rovers hits #7
Sudanese government is overthrown in a military coup
The Provisional Irish Republican Army throw a time bomb into Springfield Road British Army base in Belfast, killing British Army Sergeant Michael Willetts and wounding seven officers
USSR performs nuclear test at the Semipalatinsk Test Site in northeast Kazakhstan
US performs nuclear test at Nevada Test Site
26th Cannes Film Festival: "The Hireling" directed by Alan Bridges and "Scarecrow" directed by Jerry Schatzberg jointly awarded the Grand Prix du Festival International du Film
Peronist Hector Campora installed as President of Argentina
US launches 1st Skylab crew Kerwin, Conrad, Weitz
Cleveland's Dennis Eckersley, debuts shutting out the A's 6-0
"Beatles Live! At Star-Club in Hamburg Germany" released
Dutch social democratic party wins parliamentary election
US performs nuclear test at Nevada Test Site
Stanley Cup Final, Boston Garden, Boston, MA: 3-peat for Montreal Canadiens; beat Boston Bruins, 4-1 as Mario Tremblay scores twice; 4-2 series victory
American Airlines DC-10 crashes on takeoff from Chicago killing 273 including 2 on the ground
Israel begins to return Sinai to Egypt
Raul Gonzales of Mexico completes 50,000 m walk in record 3:41:38.4
Jacek Wszoka of Poland sets high jump record (7'8")
Daniel Goodwin scales outside of Chicago's Sears Tower in 7 hours
Rangers' Bill Stein sets AL record with 7 consecutive pinch hits
Iranian troops reconquer Khorramshar
STS-4 vehicle moves to launch pad
1st US National Missing Children's Day is proclaimed
Fire in Nassermeer, Egypt, kills 357
France performs nuclear test
Kirk Gibson (Tigers) & Jorge Orta (Blue Jays) hit inside park HRs
Detroit Tigers lose to Seattle 7-3 ends record tying 17 consecutive wins
Red Sox trade pitcher Dennis Eckersley to Cubs for Bill Buckner
Cyclone ravages Bangladesh; 11,000 killed
95-year-old woman scores a hole-in-one in Florida
Ferry boat Shamia sinks on Maghna River Bangladesh, 600 killed
Hands Across America - 6.5 million people hold hands from California to NY
Virgilio Barco elected president of Colombia
Eastern Airlines graduates it 1st class of non-union pilots
Mariners trade Mark Langston to Montreal for Randy Johnson
Stanley Cup Final, Montreal Forum, Montreal, Quebec: Calgary Flames beat Montreal Canadiens, 4-2 to win series 4 games to 2; Flames' first SC title
"People Are Still Having Sex" by LaTour hits #35
Israel evacuates 14,000 Ethiopian Jews
Stanley Cup Final, Metropolitan Sports Center, Bloomington, MN: Pittsburgh Penguins beat Minnesota North Stars, 8-0 for a 4-2 series win; Penguins' first Final series appearance
NY Yankees score 9 runs before 1st out in 8th inn, beat Brewers 13-7
Oscar Luigi Scalfaro elected President of Italy
The Bosnian Serb Army kills 72 youngsters in the Bosnian city of Tuzla
1st Super Rugby Final, Eden Park, Auckland: Blues win inaugural title with a 45-21 victory over the Natal Sharks; fullback Adrian Cashmore lands 3 penalties & 3 conversions for the home team
Military coup d'etat in Sierra Leone replaces President Ahmad Tejan Kabbah with army Major Johnny Paul Koromah; Kabbah restored to office after 9 months
The United States House of Representatives releases the Cox Report detailing People's Republic of China's nuclear espionage against the U.S. over prior two decades
Liberation Day of Lebanon. Israel withdraws its army from most of the Lebanese territory after 22 years of its first invasion in 1978.
32-year-old Erik Weihenmayer, of Boulder, Colorado, becomes the first blind person to reach the summit of Mount Everest
54th Cannes Film Festival: "The Son's Room" directed by Nanni Moretti wins the Palme d'Or
A train crash in Tenga, Mozambique, kills 197 people
China Airlines Flight 611: A Boeing 747-200 breaks apart in mid-air and plunges into the Taiwan Strait killing 225 people

Super Rugby Final, Jade Stadium, Christchurch: Crusaders win 4th title with a 31-13 thumping of the ACT Brumbies; flyhalf Andrew Mehrtens boots 3 penalties, 2 conversions and a dropped goal for the winners
56th Cannes Film Festival: "Elephant" directed by Gus Van Sant wins the Palme d'Or
Néstor Kirchner becomes President of Argentina after defeating Carlos Menem. He is the first elected President since the economic crisis.
61st Cannes Film Festival: "The Class" directed by Laurent Cantet wins the Palme d'Or
North Korea conducts its second nuclear test while also conducting several missile tests
Thailand’s economy shrank more than expected in the first quarter of 2009, contracting the most in a decade and plunging the nation into recession
Up to 116 people are massacred, including women and children, by the Syrian army in Houla, in the Homs province
17 children are killed by a gas cylinder explosion on a school bus in Gujrat, Pakistan
Yuichiro Miura of Japan becomes the oldest person to climb Mount Everest at 80
67th Cannes Film Festival: "Winter Sleep" directed by Nuri Bilge Ceylan wins the Palme d'Or
Dalia Grybauskaitė is re-elected President of Lithuania
Pitched battles between Islamic State-linked militants and Philippine government troops in and around Marawi, leave 43 dead, with thousands fleeing
Barbados elects its first female Prime Minister Mia Mottley, who leads the Barbados Labour Party to victory
72nd Cannes Film Festival: Bong Joon-ho's South Korean film "Parasite" wins the Palme d'Or, Mati Diop, first black woman in competition wins Grand Prix award for "Atlantics"
Boat capsizes on Lake Mai-Ndombe, western Democratic Republic of Congo, drowning at least 45 with 200 missing
First ever albino panda footage in the wild released (taken in April) from China's Wolong National Nature Reserve
Tornado tears through El Reno, Oklahoma. killing two and injuring dozens
Coup in Mali as military Colonel Assimi Goïta ousts country's civilian President Bah Ndaw and Prime Minister Moctar Ouane arresting and detaining them
New research show impact of 'climate racism' in the US, with black people facing double (+3.12C) the amount of heat stress in urban areas
US CDC says half of all US adults are now fully vaccinated, with 61% having had their first shot
Russian forces pound eastern Ukrainian cities of Sievierodonetsk and Lysychansk in attempt to take whole province of Luhansk, with at least 150 deaths

Oath Keepers militia founder Stewart Rhodes sentenced to 18 years in prison for organizing Jan 6 attack on the US Capitol - first person to be sentenced for seditious conspiracy and longest sentence so far
US population aged at its fastest rate for 130 years, over 65 years olds grew by a third (2010 - 2020), median age of an American jumps from 37.2 to 38.8, according to new census figures
77th Cannes Film Festival: American director Sean Baker's "Anora" wins the Palme d'Or
Severe storms across Texas, Oklahoma, Arkansas, Missouri, Kentucky, Arkansas and Alabama bring tornadoes and high winds causing at least 26 deaths
Formula 1 driverLando Norriswins the Monaco Grand Prix for McLaren, the team's first victory at the event since 2008
PopePius Vforms The Holy League to fight the Ottomans with other Catholic maritime states including Spain, Venice, Naples and Sicily
Albrecht von Wallenstein's Catholic League army recaptures Prague
Richard Cromwellresigns as Lord Protector of England, earning the nickname "Tumbledown Dick" as a result of his abrupt fall from power
KingCharles IIof England lands at Dover harbour on his way to London during the Restoration
Constitutional convention opens at Philadelphia,George Washingtonpresiding
Collection of poems bySamuel Taylor Coleridgepublished by John Murray in London, including "Kubla Khan" and "Christabel"
Christian Dopplerpresents his idea, now known as the Doppler Effect (through the changing colors of binary stars), to the Royal Bohemian Society, Prague
First Battle of Winchester, Virginia:Stonewall Jackson's Confederate forces achieve major victory
US Civil War: Battle of New Hope Church, Georgia: MajorWilliam T. Sherman's Union forces launch attack but suffers heavy losses and entrenchment on both sides
W. S. GilbertandArthur Sullivan's comic opera "H.M.S. Pinafore" premieres in London, their first international success
Oscar Wildesentenced to 2 years imprisonment for gross indecency
Revolution in Mexico overthrows long-time PresidentPorfirio Díaz
A British Cabinet meeting at 10 Downing Street on the failure of the Dardanelles offensive sacksLord Kitcheneras head of munitions andWinston Churchillas Lord of the Admiralty
Casey Stengelreleases a sparrow from under his baseball cap
Babe Ruthsuspended 1 day and fined $200 for throwing dirt on an umpire
Béla Bartók's "Cantata Profana - The Nine Enchanted Stags" premieres in London, England, with the BBC Symphony Orchestra and Wireless Chorus conducted by Aylmer Buesst
Ted Williamsraises his batting average over .400 for 1st time in 1941
Agnes Mooreheadappears in "Sorry, Wrong Number" on the radio program "Suspense", her most successful appearance
WWII: Yugoslav guerrilla movement Partisan leader JosipTitoescapes Germans surrounding Bosnia
Polish war heroWitold Pileckiis executed by communist police after a show trial in Warsaw
New York Giants outfielderWillie Mays(20) plays 1st major league game, an 8-5 win over the Phillies at Shibe Park in Philadelphia; he bats 0 for 5
PopePius XIIpublishes encyclical Haurietis aquas
Legendary rugby playerColin Meadsmakes his All Black debut against Australia - New Zealand win 25-11 in Sydney
Soviet leaderNikita Khrushchevvisits Angola
JFKannounces the US goal of putting a man on the Moon before the end of the decade
NASA civilian pilotJoseph A. Walkertakes X-15 to 32,770 m
16th Emmy Awards: "The Dick Van Dyke Show",Dick Van Dyke&Mary Tyler Moorewin
John Lennontakes delivery of his psychedelically painted Rolls Royce
"Midnight Cowboy" directed by John Schlesinger and starringJon VoightandDustin Hoffmanis released (Academy Awards Best Picture 1970)
HeavyweightJoe FrazierKOs Ron Stander
George Harrisonreleases single "Give Me Love" in UK
"Brady BunchHour" last airs on ABC-TV
Original "Star Wars" movie (Episode IV – A New Hope), directed byGeorge Lucasand starringMark Hamill,Carrie FisherandHarrison Ford, premieres
Horror film "Alien", directed byRidley Scottand starringSigourney Weaveris released
RCA releasesDavid Bowie's 13th studio album, "Lodger"; his third collaboration with Brian Eno in what becomes known as his 'Berlin trilogy' features contributions from guitarist Adrian Belew
Boston Red SoxCarl Yastrzemski(41) is 4th to play 3,000 MLB baseball games (Cobb, Musial & Aaron)
Ferguson Jenkinsbecomes 7th pitcher to strike out 3,000 batters
"Return of the Jedi" (Star WarsEpisode VI), produced byGeorge Lucasfirst released
KC RoyalGeorge Brettgets his 2,000th hit
Mikhail Gorbachevelected Executive President in the Soviet Union
Weird Al Yankovicrecords parody song "She Drives Like Crazy"
Jay Lenobecomes permanent host of US talk show "The Tonight Show"
Minnesota Twins retireKirby Puckett's uniform #
Todd Stottlemyre andMel Stottlemyrebecome first father and son to win 100 games in Major League Baseball
53rd Cannes Film Festival: "Dancer in the Dark" directed by Lars von Trier and starringBjörk(Best Actress) wins the Palme d'Or
Laureus World Sports Awards, Monte-Carlo Sporting, Monaco: Sportsman:Tiger Woods; Sportswoman:Marion Jones(rescinded); Team: Manchester United FC
Oprah Winfreyairs her last show, ending her twenty five year run of The Oprah Winfrey Show
ASpaceXDragon becomes the first commercial spacecraft to dock at the International Space Station
Petro Poroshenkois elected President of Ukraine
"Wonder Woman" directed by Patty Jenkins, starringGal Gadotand Chris Pine premieres in Los Angeles - 1st superhero film directed by a woman
Facebook'sMark Zuckerbergreceives an honorary degree from Harvard University, after dropping out in 2004
Film producerHarvey Weinsteinturns himself in to New York police to face charges of rape, a criminal sex act, sex abuse and sexual misconduct

Video of African AmericanGeorge Floyd's arrest and murder while restrained in Minneapolis police custody shows he was pinned to the ground by police officer Derek Chauvin's knee for 8 minutes and 46 seconds, igniting widespread condemnation and nationwide protests
Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesusre-elected Director-General of the World Health Organization in Geneva, Switzerland
Edict of Worms outlawsMartin Lutherand his followers
Henry Fordannounces that he is ending production of the Model T Ford
Legendary American athleteJesse Owensequals or breaks four world records in 45 minutes at a Big Ten meet at Ferry Field in Ann Arbor, Michigan; remembered as "the greatest 45 minutes ever in sport"
Muhammad AliKOs Sonny Liston at 2:12 of round 1 at Central Maine Civic Center, Lewiston to retain his WBC/WBA heavyweight boxing title
The Ostankino Tower in Moscow catches fire for the second time
Emperor Shenzong of China, 6th emperor of the Song dynasty in China, born in Kaifeng, China (d. 1085)
Sukō, 3rd Japanese Emperor of the Northern Court (1348-51), born in Japan (d. 1398)
Mahmud Begada, Sultan of Gujarat (successfully captured Pavagadh and Junagadh forts in battles), born in Ahmedabad, India (d. 1511)
Jacopo da Pontormo [Carucci], Italian painter (Sepulture of Christ), born in Pontormo, Italy (d. 1557)
Camillus de Lellis, Italian Roman Catholic saint (founder of the Ministers of the Sick), born in Bucchianico, Kingdom of Naples (d. 1614)
Charles Garnier, French Jesuit missionary in New France, born in Paris (d. 1649)
Claude Buffier, French philosopher and historian, born in Warsaw, Poland (d. 1737)
Christian August Jacobi, German composer, born in Grimma, Leipzig, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1725)
John Stuart, 3rd earl of Bute, Prime Minister of Great Britain (1760-63), born in Parliament Square, Edinburgh, Midlothian (d. 1792)
Samuel Ward, American politician (31st and 33rd Governor of the Colony of Rhode Island and Providence Plantations), born in Newport, Rhode Island (d. 1776)
Giuseppe Paolucci, Italian composer, born in Siena, Grand Duchy of Tuscany (d. 1776)
Jean de Neufville, Dutch-American merchant (started 4th English war), born in Amsterdam (d. 1796)
Friedrich Johann Eck, German violinist and composer (Concertos pour violon), born in Mannheim, Electorate of the Palatinate, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1838)
Augustin Daniel Belliard, French general who fought in the French Revolutionary and Napoleonic Wars, born in Fontenay-le-Comte, Kingdom of France (d. 1832)[1]
Hendrik Marcus de Kock, Dutch general and politician (Lieutenant Governor-General of the Dutch East Indies 1826-30), born in Heusden, Dutch Republic (d. 1845)
Ferdinand Karl Joseph, Archduke of Austria-Este, born in Milan (d. 1850)
Philip Pendleton Barbour, American politician and U.S. Supreme Court justice, born in Gordonsville, Virginia (d. 1841)
Simon Paap, Dutch dwarf (71 cm/28 inches tall) cabaret artist, born in Zandvoort, Holland (d. 1828)
Henry Hopkins Sibley, American career officer, commander of a Confederate cavalry brigade (Civil War), born in Natchitoches, Louisiana (d. 1886)
Jacob Burckhardt, Swiss cultural historian, born in Basel, Switzerland (d. 1897)
Diederich Krug, German pianist and composer (Le petit Chevalier), born in Hamburg, German Confederation (d. 1880)
Jules de Geyter, Belgian poet (International), born in Lede, Belgium (d. 1905)
John Eliot, English meteorologist, born in Lamesley, Durham (d. 1908)
Lip Pike, American baseball player, born in New York City (d. 1893)
Naim Frashëri, Albanian poet and writer, born in Frashër, Janina Vilayet, Ottoman Empire (d. 1900)
Princess Helena of the United Kingdom, daughter of Queen Victoria, born in Buckingham Palace, London (d. 1923)
Alphonse Goovaerts, Belgian composer and musicologist (Histoire et Bibliographie de la Typographie Musicale dans les Pays-Bas), born in Antwerp, Belgium (d. 1922)
John Alexander Dowie, Scottish-American evangelist (founded the city of Zion, Illinois), born in Edinburgh, Scotland (d. 1907)
Johann Baptist Singenberger, Swiss composer, music teacher, editor and publisher of liturgical music, born in St. Gallen, Switzerland (d. 1924)
"Blind" Tom Wiggins, African-American pianist and composer, born in Harris County, Georgia (d. 1908)[1][2]
William Muldoon, American wrestler and owner of NY health institute "The Olympia", born in Caneadea, Allegany County, New York (d. 1933)
Louis Franchet d'Espèrey, French general during WWI, born in Mostaganem, French Algeria (d. 1942)
James McKeen Cattell, American psychologist, born in Easton, Pennsylvania (d. 1944)
Frederick Augustus III, King of Saxony (1904-18), born in Dresden, Kingdom of Saxony (d. 1932)
John Mott, American evangelist and organizer (YMCA, Nobel 1946), born in Livingston Manor, Sullivan County, New York (d. 1955)
Pieter Zeeman, Dutch physicist (Zeeman effect, Nobel Prize 1902), born in Zonnemaire, Schouwen-Duiveland (d. 1943)
Billy Murray, Irish-American vaudeville and early recording popular music tenor, known as the 'Denver Nightengale' ("Shine On, Harvest Moon"), born in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania (d. 1954)
William Maxwell Aitken, Lord Beaverbrook, Canadian English newspaper publisher (Daily Express), born in Maple, Ontario (d. 1964)
Jean Alexandre Barré, French neurologist who helped identify the Guillain-Barré-Strohl syndrome, born in Nantes, France (d. 1967)
Harry Fox [Arthur Carringford], American vaudeville dancer, comedian and actor (Talent Scout), born in Pomona, California (d. 1959)
Marie Doro, American stage and film actress of the early silent film era (The Heart of Nora Flynn, The White Pearl), born in Duncannon, Pennsylvania (d. 1956)
Elsa Maxwell, American writer (Jack Paar Show), born in Keokuk, Iowa (d. 1963)
Philip Murray, 1st president of the United Steelworkers and longest-serving president of the Congress of Industrial Organizations, born in Blantyre, Scotland (d. 1952)
Rash Behari Bose, Indian revolutionary leader against the British Raj in India, born in Bengal, India (d. 1945)
Padre Pio [Francesco Forgione], Italian priest, Capuchin friar, and Roman Catholic saint, born in Pietrelcina, Benevento, Kingdom of Italy (d. 1968)
Miles Malleson, British stage and screen actor, and writer (Kind Hearts and Coronets; Postman's Knock), born in Croydon, Surrey, England (d. 1969)
Gilardo Gilardi, Argentine composer, pianist and conductor (La leyenda del urutaú; Réquiem; Misa de Gloria), born in San Fernando, Argentina (d. 1963)
Sverre Jordan, Norwegian composer, orchestra conductor and pianist (Norwegian Rapsody), born in Bergen, Norway (d. 1972)
Robert Willem Pieter Peereboom, Dutch editor in chief (Haarlem Newspaper), born in Haarlem, North Holland, Netherlands (d. 1956)
(Ernest) "Pop" Stoneman, American country music singer-songwriter, guitarist, and autoharp player ("Sinking of the Titanic"; The Stoneman Family), born in Monarat, Virginia (d. 1968)
Dirk Vansina, Flemish playwright (Verschaeve Gives Evidence), born in Antwerp, Belgium (d. 1967)
Jan Nicolaas Bakhuizen van den Brink, Dutch theologist and church historian, born in Joure, Netherlands (d. 1987)
Alan Kippax, Australian cricket batsman (22 Tests, 2 x 100, 8 x 50, HS 145; NSW CA), born in Sydney, Australia (d. 1972)
Bennett Cerf, American publisher (Random House) and panelist (What's My Line), born in Manhattan, New York (d. 1971)
Gene Tunney, American boxer (world heavyweight champion 1926-30), born in New York City (d. 1978)
Gustav Regler, German writer, born in Merzig (d. 1963)
Mischa Levitzki, Russian-born American pianist and composer (The Enchanted Nymph), born in Kremenchuk, Russian Empire (d. 1941)
Kazi Nazrul Islam, Bengali poet, musician, revolutionary and philosopher, born in Asansol, Burdwan, Bengal Presidency, British India (d. 1976)
Alain Grandbois, French Canadian poet (Né à Québec: Louis Jolliet), born in Saint-Casimir, Quebec (d. 1975)
Milenko Živković, Serbian composer, conductor, and pedagogue, born in Belgrade, Kingdom of Serbia (d. 1964)
Helvi Leiviskä, Finnish composer, born in Helsinki, Finland (d. 1982)
Kurt Thomas, German composer, born in Tönning, Germany (d. 1973)
Joseph C. Harsch, American newscaster (Background), born in Toledo, Ohio (d. 1998)
Martín Dihigo, Cuban Baseball HOF two-way player (2 × Negro League All-Star New York Cubans; 4 × Cuban League MVP), born in Matanzas, Cuba (d. 1971)
Theodore Roethke, American poet (Words for the Wind), born in Saginaw, Michigan (d. 1963)
Alfred Kubel, German politician, born in Braunschweig, Germany (d. 1999)
Marie Menken, American experimental filmmaker and socialite, born in Brooklyn, New York (d. 1970)
Eddie Maxwell [Cherkose], American lyricist (Breathless) and actor, born in Detroit, Michigan (d. 1999)
Princess Deokhye of Korea, last princess of the Korean Empire, born in Changdeok Palace, Keijo, Japanese Korea (d. 1989)
(Virginia) "Ginny" Simms, American singer (Kay Kyser Band), actress (Night and Day), and radio star, born in San Antonio, Texas (d. 1994)[1]
J. Peter Grace, American businessman and industrialist, born in Manhasset, New York (d. 1995)
Richard Dimbleby, British journalist and broadcaster, born in Richmond, Surrey, England (d. 1965)

Dorothy Sarnoff, American operatic soprano, Broadway actress (Rosalinda), author and pioneer of the self-help movement (Speech Can Change Your Life, Speech Dynamics), born in Brooklyn, New York (d. 2008)
Dan Wolf, American journalist, writer, and editor (founder of The Village Voice), born in Upper West Side, New York (d. 1996)
Lou van Rees, Dutch impresario, born in The Hague, Netherlands (d. 1993)
Jimmy Hamilton, American jazz saxophonist, born in Dillon, South Carolina (d. 1994)
Steve Cochran [Robert Alexander Cochran], American actor (Mozambique, Gay Senotiys, Dallas), born in Eureka, California (d. 1965)
Johnny Beazley, American MLB baseball pitcher (1941-42 & 1946-49 (St. Louis Cardinals - winner of two 1942 World Series games; Boston Braves), and WWII veteran, born in Nashville, Tennessee (d. 1990)
Peder Lunde Sr., Norwegian yachtsman (Olympic silver 1952), born in Nordstrand, Norway (d. 2009)
Gino Negri, Italian composer, songwriter, and actor, born in Perledo, Lombardy, Italy (d. 1991)
Lindsey Nelson, American sportscaster (NY Mets), born in Pulaski, Tennessee (d. 1995)

(Catherine) "Kitty" Kallen, American big band (Harry James, Jimmy Dorsey, Jack Teagarden) and pop singer ("Little Things Mean A Lot"; "My Coloring Book"), born in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania (d. 2016)[1]
Hal David, American lyricist ("Raindrops Keep Fallin' On My Head"; "What The World Needs Now Is Love"; "Do You Know the Way to San Jose"), born in Brooklyn, New York (d. 2012)
Jack Steinberger, American physicist (1988 Nobel Prize for Physics - Neutrinos), born in Bad Kissingen, Germany (d. 2020)[1]
Donald McPherson, American WWII naval fighter pilot and flying ace, born in Adams, Nebraska (d. 2025)[1]
John Weitz, American author and fashion designer (Friends in High Places), born in Berlin, Germany (d. 2002)
István Nyers, Hungarian footballer, born in Freyming-Merlebach, Moselle, France (d. 2005)
Marshall Allen, American avant garde jazz saxophonist and band leader (Sun Ra Arkestra), born in Louisville, Kentucky[1]
Aldo Clementi, Italian composer (Collage), and teacher (University of Bologna, 1971-92), born in Catania, Sicily (d. 2011)
Don Liddle, American baseball player, born in Mount Carmel, Illinois (d. 2000)
Jeanne Crain, American actress (Man Without a Star, Pinky), born in Barstow California (d. 2003)
Rosario Castellanos, Mexican poet (Balún Canán), born in Mexico City (d. 1974)
Claude Akins, American character actor (B. J. and the Bear, The Misadventures of Sheriff Lobo, Movin' On), born in Nelson, Georgia (d. 1994)
Norman Petty, American musician, songwriter, recording studio owner, and record producer (Buddy Holly), born in Clovis, New Mexico (d. 1984)
Robert Ludlum, American spy novelist (Bourne Identity), born in New York City (d. 2001)
Frigyes Hidas, Hungarian composer, and musical director (Hungarian National Theater, 1951-66; Budapest Operetta, 1974-79), born in Budapest, Hungary (d. 2007)
Beverly Sills [Belle Miriam Silverman], American operatic soprano, born in Brooklyn, New York (d. 2007)
David Sturtevant Ruder, American lawyer, college professor, and 23rd chairman of Security & Exchange Commission (1987-89), born in Wausau, Wisconsin (d. 2020)
Warren Frost, American actor (Twin Peaks, Matlock, Seinfeld), born in Newburyport, Massachusetts (d. 2017)
Sonia Rykiel, French fashion designer, born in Paris, France (d. 2016)
John Gabriel [Jack Monkarsh], American stage and screen actor (Ryan's Hope), singer, lyricist, and producer, born in Niagara Falls, New York (d. 2021)
Georgy Grechko, Soviet cosmonaut (Soyuz 17, 26, T-14), born in Leningrad, Soviet Union (d. 2017)
